coredata.dc:title: Metallurgical pyrolysis toward Co@Nitrogen-doped carbon composite for lithium storage
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanyan
Sun, Kang
Jiang, Jianchun
Zhou, Wenshu
Shang, Yuan
Du, Chenxia
Li, Baojun
coredata.dc:description: Elemental state matter-heteroatom-doped carbon composites are of great importance for the development of anode in lithium ion batteries (LIBs). In this article, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are adopted as precursor to prepare Co composites via metallurgical pyrolysis under controllable conditions. The obtained nitrogen-doped porous carbon-Co nanocomposite possesses core–shell structure (Co@C–N). Co@C–N exhibits the best Li storage performances as anode active matter. After the 200th cycles at current density of 0.2 A g−1, a reversible capacity of 870 mAh g−1 is retained. A reversible capacity of 275 mAh g−1 still maintains with 5 A g−1. Co@C–N presents a high reversible capacity with excellent cycle stability. Considering the corresponding experimental and theoretical results, the Co0-based N-doped porous carbon composite is proposed to work as LIBs anode matter. These results provide a new design idea for electrode matters of metallic ion battery, and demonstrate that MOFs pyrolysis is an effective method for the construction of elemental state anode materials.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co nanoparticles (NPs)
Core–shell structure
Lithium ion battery anode
Metal–organic framework (MOF)
Nitrogen-doped carbon

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The route for synthesis of various samples from Co-MOFs.
TEM images of (a–c) Co@C–N, the inset in b is the particle size distribution plot, (d) the carbon from Co@C–N after acid leaching treatment, and the SEM and elemental mapping images of (e) Co@C–N.

(a, b) XRD patterns and Raman spectra, (c, d) fine structure of C 1s and Co 2p XPS spectra of Co@C–N, (e) Nitrogen adsorption (close symbol)–desorption (open symbol) isotherms, and (f) pore-size distributions of Co@C–N, Co@C, B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4.

(a) In-situ CV curves of Co@C–N with scanning rate of 0.1 mV s−1, (b, c) discharge–charge curves of Co@C–N at various current densities, (d) rate performances, and (e) the charge (close symbol) and discharge (open symbol) performances of Co@C–N, Co@C, B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 at 0.2 A g−1.

EIS spectra of (a) Co@C–N, Co@C, B–Co3O4, and H–Co3O4, (b) Co@C–N initial, after rate performance, and after 200 cycles at 0.2 A g−1, tested in the charging state, in-situ CV curves of Co@C–N after (c) rate performance test, (d) 200 cycles at 0.2 A g−1, the charge (open symbol) and discharge (close symbol) performances of (e) Co@C–N after current rate tests, and (f) at 2 A g−1 and 5 A g−1, respectively.
The (a–h) crystal structures of LixCoy predicted by our calculation. Li and Co atoms are marked by green and blue, respectively. The formulas and space groups are indicated below each structure.

head.ce:title: Metallurgical pyrolysis toward Co@Nitrogen-doped carbon composite for lithium storage

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Elemental state matter-heteroatom-doped carbon composites are of great importance for the development of anode in lithium ion batteries (LIBs). In this article, metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) are adopted as precursor to prepare Co composites via metallurgical pyrolysis under controllable conditions. The obtained nitrogen-doped porous carbon-Co nanocomposite possesses core–shell structure (Co@C–N). Co@C–N exhibits the best Li storage performances as anode active matter. After the 200th cycles at current density of 0.2 A g−1, a reversible capacity of 870 mAh g−1 is retained. A reversible capacity of 275 mAh g−1 still maintains with 5 A g−1. Co@C–N presents a high reversible capacity with excellent cycle stability. Considering the corresponding experimental and theoretical results, the Co0-based N-doped porous carbon composite is proposed to work as LIBs anode matter. These results provide a new design idea for electrode matters of metallic ion battery, and demonstrate that MOFs pyrolysis is an effective method for the construction of elemental state anode materials.

Co based metal–organic frameworks are used as pyrolysis precursor to prepare nitrogen-doped carbon-Co composites possessing core–shell@sheet structure. The experimental results, combining with the theoretical calculation, demonstrate an effective metallurgical method for the construction of elemental state Li storage materials.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: The serious environmental problems in the using process of the dwindling fossil energy sources require new and high efficient energy storage and conversion systems. The developing smart and intelligent portable electrical devices need batteries and super capacitors of high energy and power density. The lithium ion-based batteries and super capacitors have shown somewhat outstanding performances and excellent prospects [1–3]. The novel electrode active matters are of extreme importance for the further improvement of actual energy and power density, as well as recyclability of energy storage devices. To rationally design new electrode materials, the exploration of active components and composite structures are the main direction and promisingly effective approach. A large number of new components have been tentatively suggested as potential active matters with enhanced electrochemical properties. These new alternative projects are divided generally into two categories: compounds and elemental substances. Because the compounds as active matter generally perform their lithiation-delithiation through chemical routes, serious irreversible capacity loss and low initial Coulomb efficiency (CE) are often inevitable and undesirable phenomenon. These deficiencies have been becoming a great challenging research focus [4–6]. In order to address these notorious problem, some elemental substances exhibit somewhat promising potential. The main feature of elemental substances is that their Li storage processes often involve physical alloying-dealloying process without severe irreversible formation of new compounds or irreversible capacity loss. It is responsible for some useless weight for devices [7–10]. This unique feature bestows elemental substances charm for the future design and development of high performance electrode active matter. The exploration and optimization of new elemental substances are becoming a reliable approach to superior recyclability with high initial CE. Of course, the nanoparticulation of elemental substances is also a very effective strategy for high capacity. The carbon wrapping and core-shells constructing strategy is still of importance for further improvement of electrode performances via buffering the severe volumetric expansion and contraction in the alloying-dealloying processes [11,12]. Recently, a new efficient method to enhance the kinetic of Li storage process has been demonstrated through fabrication of N-doped carbon materials [13–15]. The introduction of 2D carbon nanosheets also have been used to modulate the electrochemical properties of electrodes in batteries and super capacitors. For an elemental substance-based composite electrode material, those aforementioned strategies should be adopted simultaneously in an integrate synthesis route. The developments in inorganic coordination chemistry provide some new opportunities to find an ideal solution.

Because of the unique ordered structure of metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) composed of organic linkages and metal ion node points, MOFs hold a promise as pyrolysis precursors to fabricate composite materials, such as porous carbons, metal or metal oxides, and their assembly [16–19]. Besides a few advantages, some drawbacks exist in this synthesis strategy through MOFs pyrolysis. The low controllability is a main problem in developing effective approach for significant performance improvement. The metal species generally will catalyze the evolution of organic residual to carbon species and nanostructures (carbon nanotubes, graphene sheets, onion carbon, as well as carbon shells) [20–23]. The high catalytic activity of metal and corresponding unordered growth of carbon structures in open space causes difficulties for the successful fabrication of monodispersive composite materials. Fortunately, the catalytic activity and reactivity of metal species are definitely influenced by the reaction atmosphere and temperature program. The reaction progress and products of MOFs pyrolysis will be tuned via adjusting the redox properties of gas flow and temperature program or range. Many reported programs for pyrolysis are carried out in single atmosphere and provided simple composite products [24,25]. The abundant possibility in atmosphere and temperature program still have not attracted sufficient attention for tuning the components, morphologies and structures. The inert, oxidation and mixed atmosphere are typical environments for material preparation and post-treatment [26]. The effects of these parameters on products are interesting and worth to be investigated to achieve the goal of discovering novel elemental state electrode matter for highly efficient Li storage technology.

In this article, the calcinations of Co-MOFs are performed for controllable metallurgical pyrolysis to generate Co nanoparticles@N-doped carbon composite material (Co@C–N). Excellent lithium storage ability with outstanding current rate performance is observed. The high specific capacity comes from the reaction process between Co compounds and Li ions. These electrochemical properties are attributed to the synergy effect between Co nanoparticles (NPs) cores and N-doped carbon shells. These results demonstrate that controllable pyrolysis of MOFs is an effective strategy for construction of functional materials.

The Co-MOFs were prepared by following a literature with modification [27]. Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O (2.328 g) were dissolved in ethanol (20 mL), and 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (3.342 g) were dissolved in ethanol (80 mL) under heating at 80 °C. Then the above solutions were mixed together quickly and left stirring for 5 min. After that, the mixed solution was put into an autoclave (0.15 L), heated to 85 °C, and kept for 48 h. After being naturally cooled to room temperature, the obtained purple powders (3.240 g) were separated by centrifugations (5000 rpm, 2 min), washed with DMF (2 × 15 mL) and ethanol (2 × 20 mL), dried under 50 °C for 10 h in air.

The as-synthesized Co-MOFs (1.600 g) were ground and placed in a furnace at 700 °C for 3 h in N2 with a heating rate of 3 °C min−1. After being cooled to room temperature, the obtained black powder was directly put in ethanol (30 mL) to avoid spontaneous combustion. Then it was dried at 30 °C for 12 h, and the nitrogen-doped porous carbon-Co NPs composites (0.740 g, denoted as Co@C–N) were produced. For comparison, the mixture of Co(HCOO)2 · 2H2O (1.500 g) and glucose anhydrous (0.915 g) were calcinated using the same procedures as described above, and the porous carbon with Co NPs composites (0.805 g, denoted as Co@C) were produced. Co-MOFs (1.600 g) were annealed at 700 °C for 3 h in air with a heating rate of 3 °C min−1, and the bulk Co3O4 cube (0.520 g, denoted as B–Co3O4) were prepared. While, Co-MOFs (1.600 g) firstly was heated at 700 °C for 3 h in N2 atmosphere, then further calcinated at 300 °C for 3 h in air atmosphere, the hollow Co3O4 (0.581 g, denoted as H–Co3O4) was obtained.

The morphology of composite materials were explored by transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G [2] F20 S-TWIN electron microscope) operated at 200 kV and scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Ultra 55) combined with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on Bruker D8 advance with Cu Kα, λ = 1.5418 Å. The Raman spectra were executed by applying Renishaw RM-2000 with an Ar-ion laser (λ = 514 nm, power = 50 mW). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were researched by using a PHI quantera SXM spectrometer (Al excitation source, Kα = 1486.6 eV), and the binding energies were adjusted according to C 1s peak (284.8 eV). The N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms were studied at 77 K by employing an Automated Surface Area and Pore Size Analyzer (ASAP2420-4 MP, Micromeritics, USA). Prior to the tests, the samples were degassed for 4 h at 573 K under high vacuum. The surface areas (S BET) were figured up using the Brunauere-Emmette-Teller (BET) model according to the nitrogen adsorption curves in P/P0 range of 0.05–0.35. The pore size distributions were estimated via Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) model.

The LIBs performances were determined using CR 2016 type coin half-cells assembled in an argon-filled glove box (moisture and oxygen concentrations below 0.1 ppm). The working electrode consisted of active material (80 wt%), binder (PVDF, 10 wt%) and electrical conductor (super-p, 10 wt%) on copper foil. The counter electrode was pure lithium foil, and electrolyte was LiPF6 (1.0 M) containing vinylene carbonate (2 wt%) in ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate/diethyl carbonate (1: 1: 1, volume ratio). In-situ Cyclic voltammetry (in-situ CV) (0.02 V ∼ 3.0 V) and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) (0.05 Hz ∼ 100,000 Hz potentiostatic mode with amplitude of 5 mV and offset of 0 mV) were measured using CHI660E electrochemical workstation at room temperature. The galvanostatic cycling (0.02 V ∼ 3.0 V) was measured on a NEWARE battery test system at room temperature. The weight of active material was applied to estimate the specific discharge capacity (mAh g−1) of the battery.

The structure relaxations and properties calculations were carried out by using the Density Functional Theory (DFT) method, within the Generalized Gradient Approximation of the Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof (GGA-PBE) as implemented in the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP). The cutoff energy (400 eV) for the expansion of the wave function into plane waves and Monkhorst–Pack k meshes were chosen to ensure that all the atomic positions were fully relaxed until the Gibbs free energy changes became smaller than 1 × 10−6 eV per cell.

The overall preparation route is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Co-MOFs are calcinated under controllable condition to generate different samples. The organic ligand 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic ions in Co-MOF is transformed into N-doped carbon by the oxidation of Co2+ ions in its pyrolysis process, and simultaneously Co is metallurgically generated and surrounded by in situ formed carbon shell. Thus Co@C–N possessing core–shell structure is formed successfully. Some carbon sheets are formed as a substrate to support and connect the core–shell structure. When Co-MOFs are treated in air, the organic ligand ions are converted to CO2 and NOx, meanwhile the Co is oxidized to Co3O4. In this case, B–Co3O4 cube is produced. Whereas changing the preparation procedure with two-step calcination approach (calcinated firstly in N2, then further in air), H–Co3O4 NCs are obtained due to the solid state diffusion of Co atoms.

The structural and morphological evolution process of the precursor and samples are displayed in Fig. S1 and Fig. 2 . Co-MOF showes irregular block shape and stacked each other (Figs. S1a–S1c). Core-shell on sheets structure is observed for Co@C–N (Fig. 2a–c). Uniform Co NPs are embedded in porous carbon layer shells. The most possible distribution of Co NPs is in the range of 8–10 nm (Inset in Fig. 2b). The carbon shells prevent Co NPs from aggregating, which is helpful in sample maintaining good electrochemical properties. Furthermore, the representative HRTEM image also confirms the existence of Co core and carbon shell. The resolved interplanar distances of the lattice fringes are 0.340 and 0.203 nm, corresponding to (002) planes of graphite C and (111) planes of metallic Co, respectively (Fig. 2c) [28,29]. The effective acid leaching experiment suggests that the existence of pores in hollow carbon shells anchored on carbon sheets achieves the ion diffusion and reaction (Figs. 2d, S2 and S3). The homogeneous dispersion of Co and C in composite is also demonstrated by SEM and EDX mapping images (Fig. 2e). By contrast, Co@C displays different morphological characteristics from those of Co@N–C. The big Co particles (50–100 nm) are randomly distributed on carbon sheets. The measured interplanar distance of the lattice fringes is 0.203 nm. It is indexed as the (111) planes of Co, confirming the existence of metallic Co (Fig. S1f) [29]. B–Co3O4 are aggregated and overlapped with each other (Figs. S1g–S1i). The size of them is in the range of 150–200 nm. In addition, the indicated lattice spacing is calculated to be 0.233 nm, corresponding to (222) plane of cubic Co3O4 [30]. Hollow Co3O4 nanospheres are observed clearly (Fig. S1j–S1l) [30].

The crystalline structures of the prepared samples are studied by XRD patterns (Fig. 3 a). The diffraction peaks of Co-MOF correspond to the reported pattern (Fig. S4) [27,31]. Co@C–N and Co@C exhibit similar patterns except for the intensity of XRD peaks (Fig. 3a and S5). The diffraction peaks demonstrate the presence of C and Co. The weak peaks around 25° are ascribed to the C (002) plane [32]. The presence of cubic β-Co is manifested by the peaks around 44.2°, 51.5° and 75.8° (JCPDS card no. 89-4307). These peaks imply the successful reduction of cobalt ions to a metallic state during carbonization [33]. Compared with Co@C–N, the stronger and sharper diffraction peaks of Co@C suggest the bigger crystalline size of Co NPs. The diffraction peaks of B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 prove the existence of cubic spinel phase of Co3O4 (JCPDS card No. 42-1467) (Fig. 3a) [34]. The sharper diffraction peaks of B–Co3O4 illustrate the good crystalline degree. The Raman spectra of Co@C–N and Co@C exhibit two characteristics peaks of carbon (Fig. 3b). The D bands at about 1348 cm−1 represent the disordered sp3 hybrized carbon, and G bands at about 1588 cm−1 are assigned to the graphitic sp2 hybrized carbon [35]. The I D/I G value of Co@C–N and Co@C are ca. 1.1, matching the typical feature for disordered graphitic carbons. There are characteristics peaks of surface ligand residuals (150–700 cm−1) in the Co@C–N and Co@C spectra, demonstrating the occurrence of surface oxidation in air atmosphere [36].

To better investigate the surface elemental composition of Co@C–N, XPS analysis is performed (Fig. 3c and d). The full spectrum of Co@C–N reveals the existence of Co, C, N, and O elements (Fig. S6a). The presence of C, Co and N elements confirms the successful production of Co@C–N composites, while the observation of O indicates the occurrence of surface oxidation and the formation of little amount CoOx. The C 1s spectra are interpreted into six peaks, located at 284.2, 284.8, 285.2, 285.8, 287.2 and 291.1 eV, corresponding to Co–C, CC, CN, C–C/C–O, C–N, and CO groups, respectively (Fig. 3c) [37]. The N 1s spectra are divided into three peaks with binding energies at 398.7, 400.8, and 403.0 eV, being ascribed to pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N and oxide-N in carbon shells, respectively (Fig. S6b) [38]. The contents of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N in Co@C–N sample are 59% and 34%, respectively. It is noted that pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N is beneficial to the improvement performance of LIBs [39]. The O 1s spectra displays three peaks at about 532.0, 531.0, and 529.8 eV related to CO/C–O–C, C–OH, and C–O–Co, respectively (Fig. S6c) [40]. The Co 2p spectra shows several peaks, the peak at 779.2 eV is corresponded to Co0, the peak at 782.3 eV is due to the presence of Co3+, the peaks at 780.9 eV (Co 2p3/2) and 796.4 eV (Co 2p1/2) are attributed to Co2+ (Fig. 3d) [38,41]. The existence of Co2+ and Co3+ indicates the surface oxidation or carbonization of Co to some extent. The XPS analysis detects only the surface elemental composition (depth of near 10 nm), thus Co0 signal is somewhat weaker than Co2+. This interference phenomenon of carbon shells confirms the core–shell structure of Co@C–N. Based on the TGA results in air atmosphere, the mass ratios of Co are calculated as 58.0% and 81.4% for Co@C–N and Co@C, respectively (Fig. S7) [42].

Nitrogen sorption/desorption isotherms of the samples are measured to better understand their surface area and pore size distribution (Fig. 3e and f). Co@C–N, Co@C and H–Co3O4 exhibit type IV sorption behavior with a distinct hysteresis loop, proving a typical mesoporous structure [43]. Co@C–N shows a biggest surface area (S BET) of 238 m2 g−1 with a most possible diameter of 9 nm (Fig. 3e). The type H1 hysteresis loop is due to the ordered mesoporous structure generating from the order accumulation of core-shells on carbon sheets. The high S BET afford more surface active sites for the electrochemical reactions, and the typical mesoporous structure promote the diffusion of Li+ and electrons conduction [44]. The S BET of Co@C and H–Co3O4 are 127 and 54 m2 g−1, respectively. The most possible pore diameter of Co@C and H–Co3O4 are 4 nm and 12 nm, respectively. In addition, these three samples also possess a part of meso-macro porosity (Fig. 3f). The surface area of B–Co3O4 is only 4 m2 g−1, and the nonporous structure has been verified by its TEM images.

In-situ Cyclic voltammetry (in-situ CV) of Co@C–N is conducted in the range of 0.02–3.00 V to investigate the electrochemical details (Fig. 4 a). It is clearly observed that the first CV cycle represents a special curve due to the generation of solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) film [45]. During subsequent cycles, the CV curves tend to be closely overlapped. In the discharge processes, the peaks at 1.3 V corresponded to the lithium ion insertion into the electrode matter as LiCo, whereas the peaks at 0.7 V are due to the next lithium insertion reaction and LixCo is formed. In the charge processes, the broad peaks at about 1.26 V accompany with extraction of lithium ion from the LixCo alloy to the formation of metallic Co and LiCo, while the peaks at about 2.0 V are attributed to further extraction of lithium ion from the LiCo alloy and metallic Li and Co are generated [46,47]. In addition to the first cycle, the peak intensities and integral areas in CV curves of following cycles overlap well, revealing the stable reversibility of electrode reaction [48]. Galvanostatic cycling is employed to detect the electrochemical properties of Co@C–N in 0.01–3.0 V at 0.2 A g−1 (Fig. 4b). In representative discharge–charge curves, Co@C–N shows sloping voltage (vs. capacity) profiles less than 1.5 V, similar to those of carbon electrodes in LIBs [49,50]. No obvious charge and discharge platform hints that no obvious redox reactions occur, corresponding to the LixCo alloy reaction process. The initial discharge and charge capacities of Co@C–N are 1315 and 693 mAh g−1, respectively. The initial capacity loss is corresponded to the irreversible formation of the SEI layer on electrode surface and the decomposition of the electrolyte [51,52]. The third discharge and charge capacities of Co@C–N are 748 and 740 mAh g−1, respectively, with a coulombic efficiency (CE) of 101%. After the 200 cycles, the corresponding discharge and charge capacities of Co@C–N is 869 and 871 mAh g−1, and the CE remains around 100% (Figs. 4b and S8). The results match well with that of the in-situ CV curves. The rate performance are tested to estimate the LIBs performances, with current density increasing stepwise from 0.2 to 5 A g−1 for every 10 cycles and subsequently decreased back to 0.2 A g−1 (Fig. 4c and d). The reversible specific capacities decrease with the increase of current density (Figs. S9–S12). There is a linear correlation between the available capacities and the logarithmic current densities (Fig. S10). This phenomenon may be due to that over potential generated from the low lithium ion diffusion coefficient and the severe volume changes of materials at big current density (Figs. S12 and S13). Co@C–N exhibits better rate performances than those of other samples. Co@C–N displays much higher reversible capacities than those of Co@C at every current density. Although the initial capacities of B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 are close to that of Co@C–N, their reversible capacities already come down during the first ten cycles and the final capacities at 0.2 A g−1 are much lower than original capacities. The reversible capacities of Co@C–N are found to be 760 mAh g−1 at rates of 0.2 A g−1, and the value decrease to 380 mAh g−1 at rates of 5 A g−1. The capacities of Co@C–N achieve to 880 mAh g−1 when the current density return to 0.2 A g−1. The remarkable rate capabilities of Co@C–N indicate its promising applications as a high-rate material in LIBs anode.

The cycling performances are demonstrated at 0.2 A g−1 between 0.02 and 3 V (Fig. 4e). Obviously, Co@C–N displays the best cycling properties. The initial discharge capacity of B–Co3O4 is 1040 mAh g−1, and the second value is 730 mAh g−1. Unfortunately, the capacity decreases to 483 mAh g−1 after 100th charge–discharge cycle, which is 66% of the second capacity (Fig. 4e). The initial discharge capacity of H–Co3O4 is 1056 mAh g−1, and the capacity decrease to 90 mAh g−1 after the 100 charge–discharge cycle, which is only 13% of the second capacity. The inferior performances of B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 may be attributed to the absence of carbon components, causing bad structural stability and low apparent capacities. Therefore, their electrochemical performances are relatively inferior. Co@C–N and Co@C reveal good cycling stability, but the reversible capacities of Co@C–N are 3.7 times of that of Co@C. The gradually increasing capacities of Co@C–N and Co@C may be related to the activation of porous active material during the charge–discharge process. After 200 charge–discharge cycles at 0.2 A g−1, a reversible capacity of Co@C–N attained to 869 mAh g−1. It is 112% of the second capacity and 2.3 times of the theoretical capacity of commercial graphite (372 mAh g−1). The CE of Co@C–N steadily remains about 100% except for the first cycle (56.7%) (Fig. S8). The in-situ CV curves and charging/discharging curves of Co@C, B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 electrodes have been offered in Fig. S14. The Co@C electrode exhibits similar CV curves to that of Co@C–N electrode, indicating the LixCo alloy reaction process. The CV curves of B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 electrodes display electrochemical redox reaction between Co3O4 and metallic Co. The curves of H–Co3O4 overlap severely, indicating the unstable electrode reaction. The capacity losses in the first charging/discharging curves should be related to the formation of SEI layer.

The EIS spectra of four fresh as-assembled half-cells are displayed in Fig. 5 a and b. The EIS spectra are similar to each other, containing one semicircle and a following straight line. The semicircle portion represents the reactions occurring on the electrode–electrolyte interface, reflecting the SEI resistance (R s) and charge transfer resistance (R ct). Larger diameters of the semicircle correspond to larger R ct [53,54]. Obviously, the Co@C–N electrode (26 Ω) exhibits a lower R ct than those of cells with Co@C (71 Ω), B–Co3O4 (42 Ω) and H–Co3O4 (40 Ω). The R ct of fresh, rate cycled and 200th cycled Co@C–N electrodes are 26, 42, and 46 Ω, respectively. The low total resistance reveals the efficient electronic and Li ionic conductivity of Co@C–N. The transition region from semicircle to straight line is due to the electrochemical change in crystal structure of active matter. To further research the electrochemical properties of Co@C–N, the in-situ CV curves of cells after rate performance and after 200th cycles are also measured (Fig. 5c and d). The curves display similar peaks, indicating the stable electrodes reaction. The main peaks at 0.5 V in the discharge processes are due to the lithium ion insertion into the electrode as LixCo. At the reverse scan, the broad peaks at 1.3 V belong to the extraction of lithium ions from the LixCo alloy and generation of metallic Co and Li [46,55].

The capacities of Co@C–N are evaluated after rate performance tests (Fig. 5e). It is noted that the reversible capacities reach to 890 mAh g−1 when the current density is decreased to 0.2 A g−1 after more than 200 cycles (Fig. 5e). After being tested at high currents for a long time, the available reversible capacities of Co@C–N maintain about 450 mAh g−1 at 2 A g−1 and 280 mAh g−1 at 5 A g−1 with high CE after the 500th charge–discharge cycle (Fig. 5f, S15, S16). The results hint that Co@C–N samples have potential as high-rate material in LIBs anode. Generally speaking, the anode material may disintegrate or aggregate during the repeated discharge/charge processes leading to fading performance. As discussed above, Co@C–N used as LIBs anode presents high capacity, good rate performance and cycling stability. To better interpret the properties of Co@C–N, anode materials are characterized by TEM images after being tested for 200 cycles at 0.2 A g−1 (Fig. S17). The core–shell structures of Co@C–N are well preserved and the small particle size is also maintained with no severe aggregation or collapse, confirming the stable structure of the sample. These post-mortem investigations demonstrate the merits of the well-crafted Co@C–N composite. It is beneficial for achieving excellent LIBs performances. The subsequent capacity losses of B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 electrodes reflect the instability of electrochemical process. Co@C, B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 electrode materials are characterized by TEM images after being tested 100 cycles at 0.2 A g−1 (Fig. S18). The structures of Co@C are maintained basicly, confirming the good cycle performance of Co@C electrode. The structures of B–Co3O4 and H–Co3O4 are destroyed, explaining their bad cycle performances. These post-mortem investigations demonstrate that the stability of structure affect the LIBs performances. This are beneficial for designing electrode materials.

A structural search is performed by employing a particle swarm optimization algorithm in the CALYPSO code [56]. It is able to efficiently find the stable structures just depending on the given chemical compositions. Its validity has been confirmed by the application of a diverse variety of alloy materials. The lowest energy structures of alloy LixCoy (x:y = 3:1) is found via CALYPSO code. Unit cells including Li3Co1, Li6Co2, Li9Co3, Li12Co4 formula units (f.u.) are studied. Firstly, atomic coordinates are produced through crystallographic symmetry operations to fabricate random structures with certain symmetry. The conjugate gradients method is employed to execute local optimizations via VASP code [57]. Because of lower Gibbs free energies, 60% of the first generation structures are single out to establish the next generation structures by PSO. 40% structures in the new generation are created at random. The identical structures in the generated structures are grimly prohibited through a structure fingerprinting technique of bond characterization matrix. The variety of the structures is strengthened via aforementioned procedure. Therefore the efficiency of structural global search is greatly improved. In this case, structural searching simulations are terminated after creating about 1000 structures for each calculation. A series of dynamic and thermal stable LixCoy (x:y = 3:1) structures were found through first principles swarm structural search (Fig. 6a–h). The specific capacity of Li3Co alloy obtained from the theoretical calculation is consistent with the result obtained from the experimental measurements. The simulation structure agrees with the proposed structure of Li3Co1 alloy based on the experimental data.

In all these materials, Co@C–N presents a substantially reversible capacity and good cycle stability. The reasons are concluded as follows: Co–Li alloy reaction is conducive to good electrochemical Li storage performance. The well-developed porous structure significantly shortens the ion diffusion distance. The impact of volume changes during discharge/charge processes is relieved effectively. This helps Li ions to occur reversible-embedded reaction. The pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N in framework offer more surface active sites for Li ions. And thus the transportation of electrons and Li ions in the electrode is accelerated substantially. The relatively uniform size and surface of Co NPs ensure steady electrochemical process. Carbon shells prevent Co NPs from gathering during the charging–discharging cycles, and simultaneously improve the conductivity and cycle stability of electrodes. Co@C–N exhibits some advantages in term of preparation condition and capacity. Due to the very high temperature process for preparation of high capacity anode matters, such as Si and Ge, this route provides a choice for relative low temperature operation process. Higher capacity and excellent rate performances are obtained with Co@C–N (Table S2). As a general synthesis strategy, a new avenue is opened for the pyrolysis of MOFs through providing novel elemental state anode matters.

In conclusion, nitrogen-doped porous carbon-Co NPs composites are fabricated via controllable metallurgical pyrolysis of Co-MOF. The Co0-based N-doped porous carbon composites are used as anode to storage Li ions via an experimentally and theoretically proposed reversible formation of LixCo alloy. Compared with other pyrolysis products, Co@C–N exhibits excellent lithium ion storage performances. After the 200th charge–discharge cycle at 0.2 A g−1, a reversible capacity of 870 mAh g−1 is retained. It is 112% of the second capacity. A substantially reversible capacity of about 270 mAh g−1 still is maintained at 5 A g−1. These positive results provide a new promising design idea for elemental state-matter based LIBs electrode materials. This work demonstrates that metallurgical MOFs pyrolysis is an effective method for the construction of element state anode materials with variety of morphologies and structures.
